concerns.text: The plan mandates a Monte-Carlo shuffle test where the 'solar proxy time-series is shuffled relative to the anisotropy series' to estimate FAP. This is a methodological error for testing periodicity/correlation in time-series. Shuffling the proxy destroys the temporal structure of the solar cycle (the independent variable) but leaves the anisotropy time-series (dependent variable) intact. If the anisotropy series has intrinsic autocorrelation or a trend (common in cosmic ray data), shuffling the proxy does not generate a valid null distribution for the correlation coefficient because the null hypothesis (independence) is not correctly simulated. The correct null for time-series correlation requires preserving the autocorrelation structure of *both* series (e.g., via phase randomization of both or block-shuffling both) or using a surrogate method that respects the specific null hypothesis. The current method will likely yield biased FAPs.

The plan proposes using Lomb-Scargle to detect an 11-year periodicity in a 10-year dataset (2010-2020). This is a fundamental frequency resolution violation. The frequency resolution of a periodogram is approximately 1/T, where T is the total observation time. For T=10 years, the resolution is ~0.1 cycles/year. The 11-year solar cycle corresponds to ~0.09 cycles/year. The peak of the solar cycle and the zero-frequency (DC) component will be indistinguishable or heavily aliased. A Lomb-Scargle periodogram on a 10-year window cannot statistically distinguish an 11-year cycle from a linear trend or a DC offset. The analysis is scientifically unsound for the stated goal of 'detecting 11-year periodicity' within this specific time window.

The plan defines a 'positive result' as |r| ≥ 0.4 AND p ≤ 0.0017. The threshold |r| ≥ 0.4 is an arbitrary effect-size cutoff not derived from the physics of cosmic ray modulation or the expected signal-to-noise ratio of the detectors. In high-energy astrophysics, anisotropy amplitudes are often < 0.1% (10^-3). A correlation coefficient of 0.4 implies a very strong linear relationship that may not be physically realistic for this phenomenon. More critically, combining an arbitrary effect-size threshold with a p-value threshold creates a 'double-dipping' validation risk if the threshold was tuned to a specific dataset. The threshold should be justified by power analysis or physical models, not set arbitrarily.

The plan specifies a block length of '2 × configured bin size' (e.g., 54 days for 27-day bins) for the block bootstrap. While this attempts to preserve autocorrelation, the choice of block length is critical. For a 10-year time series with ~115 points, a block of 54 days (2 bins) is extremely short and may not capture the full autocorrelation length of the solar cycle or the cosmic ray modulation. If the true autocorrelation length is longer than the block size, the bootstrap will underestimate the variance of the correlation coefficient, leading to inflated Type I error rates (false positives). The plan should justify the block length choice or implement a data-driven method (e.g., stationary bootstrap) to select it.
